title: Counting Pattern Avoiding Permutations Via Integral Operators
speaker: Richard Ehrenborg
affiliation: University of Kentucky; Institute for Advanced Study
abstract: A permutation pi=(pi_{1},...,pi_{n}) is consecutive 123-avoiding if there is no sequence of the form pi_i < pi_{i+1} < pi_{i+2}. More generally, for S a collection of permutations on m+1 elements, this definition extends to define consecutive S-avoiding permutations. We show that the spectrum of an associated integral operator on the space L^2([0,1]^m) determines the asymptotics of the number of consecutive S-avoiding permutations.

proof_stages.label: Permutation counts as cube volumes
proof_stages.summary: The count alpha_n of permutations with no consecutive 123 pattern is normalized by n! and identified with the volume of the region in the unit cube avoiding inequalities x_i<x_{i+1}<x_{i+2}.

proof_stages.label: Transfer operator for the no-double-ascent condition
proof_stages.summary: The avoidance region is encoded by an indicator function, integrated down to functions F_n of the last two variables, yielding a linear integral operator T with alpha_n/n! = <T^{n-2}1,1>.

proof_stages.label: Explicit eigenproblem for the 123 case
proof_stages.summary: The eigenvalue equation for T is split over the two triangles x<y and y<x, reduced from integral equations to a two-dimensional ODE system, and solved to obtain explicit real eigenvalues and eigenfunctions.

proof_stages.label: Compact-operator spectral expansion
proof_stages.summary: Functional analysis is invoked by observing that T^2 is Hilbert-Schmidt/compact, allowing the desired inner product <T^{n-2}1,1> to be expanded using simple eigenvalues, eigenfunctions, and adjoint eigenfunctions with a controlled remainder.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic expansion and its limitation
proof_stages.summary: Substituting the explicit eigenvalues gives finite asymptotic expansions dominated by the largest positive eigenvalue, but the coefficients for positive eigenvalues grow so the method does not yield a convergent exact infinite series for alpha_n.

proof_stages.label: General consecutive-pattern operator
proof_stages.summary: For any forbidden set S of consecutive patterns of length m+1, the reduction map Pi and an analogous indicator define a transfer operator on L^2([0,1]^m), although direct computation of its full spectrum quickly becomes intractable.

proof_stages.label: Krein-Rutman criterion via an overlap graph
proof_stages.summary: A positivity-improving theorem for compact operators is translated into ergodicity of a directed sliding-window graph H, giving a simple positive dominant eigenvalue and hence alpha_N(S) = C lambda^N plus a smaller error term in broad pattern-avoidance cases.

examples.name: Three-dimensional cube cut into six permutation simplices
examples.note: The toy 3-cube sliced by the planes x_i=x_j makes the volume-to-permutation correspondence visible, with the 123 simplex removed in the no-double-ascent case.
examples.engagement_reason: he physically cuts/describes the 3D cube by the coordinate hyperplanes, identifies the six permutation simplices, and reasons that the 123 simplex is missing

examples.name: Sequential random-variable rejection picture
examples.note: In response to a question, the operator recursion is recast as choosing uniform coordinates one at a time and rejecting a choice when it creates a double ascent.
examples.engagement_reason: he traces the random-variable construction step by step, picking coordinates and rejecting them when they create a double ascent

passes.reason: The sequential uniform-random rejection construction is an ordinary probabilistic encoding of the avoidance condition.
The sequential rejection process is an ordinary probabilistic interpretation of the integral operator, with no special structural status.

examples.name: Alternating permutations and Euler numbers comparison
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the present nonconvergent spectral expansion with the earlier alternating-permutation/Euler-number case where the same style of method yields an actual convergent series.

examples.name: Consecutive 213-avoiding permutations
examples.note: The 213 case leads to an eigenvalue equation involving the error function, visibly motivating the move from explicit eigenvalue solution to a Perron-Frobenius/Krein-Rutman leading-eigenvalue approach.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the operator/eigenvalue calculation to consecutive 213 avoidance and derives the error-function equation for its eigenvalues and root behavior

passes.reason: Consecutive 213-avoidance is a standard singleton consecutive-pattern class, used here to show the eigenvalue method already leads to non-explicit transcendental equations.
Consecutive 213-avoidance is a standard pattern-avoidance class, used to show that the spectral calculation quickly stops giving exact closed forms.
the consecutive 213-avoidance class is an ordinary singleton pattern-avoidance family but is used to show clean eigenvalue calculations break down
The consecutive 213-avoiding class is a standard singleton-pattern case used to show the nice explicit eigenvalue calculation already breaks down.
The consecutive 213-avoidance case is a typical nonmonotone pattern class used as a hard case showing exact elementary eigenvalue formulas break down.
213 is a representative nonmonotone length-three consecutive pattern, used as a harder worked case rather than a disproof
serves as an obstruction to the hope for simple exact eigenvalue formulas, while 213-avoidance is an ordinary consecutive-pattern class
The 213 consecutive-avoidance class is ordinary structurally, but is used as a hard case where the explicit 123 eigenvalue calculation breaks down.
ordinary nonmonotone consecutive pattern class used as a hard case where simple explicit eigenvalues break down
the standard consecutive 213-avoidance class is used to show the spectral calculation becomes transcendental rather than explicitly solvable

examples.name: Singleton forbidden pattern for m greater than 2
examples.note: As an application of the graph criterion, any one forbidden consecutive pattern of length m+1 with m>2 satisfies the hypotheses and hence has the asserted one-dominant-eigenvalue asymptotics.
examples.engagement_reason: he states that for m greater than 2 and a singleton forbidden set the theorem applies and proves a conjectured asymptotic form, without showing the verification
